Name: spectral_quantization_routing
Title: Spectral Quantization Routing: Training-Free Precision Allocation via Activation Covariance Geometry
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that the local covariance condition number and spectral decay rate of hidden states serve as direct proxies for quantization sensitivity, enabling training-free precision allocation and strict latency budgets.
Related Work: Our proposal builds upon recent work in spectral latent steering and geometric signal separation, but differs in its focus on training-free precision allocation and strict latency budgets. Unlike existing methods that rely on static profiling or costly calibration, our approach uses real-time activation geometry to dynamically allocate numerical precision.
Abstract: Mixed-precision inference promises significant latency and memory gains, but typically relies on static profiling or costly calibration. We propose Spectral Quantization Routing (SQR), a training-free framework that dynamically allocates numerical precision based on real-time activation geometry. By computing local covariance condition numbers and spectral decay rates, SQR maps these diagnostics to a precision-switching policy that enforces strict latency budgets. We theoretically ground SQR in PAC-Bayes bounds, linking spectral conditioning to generalization stability under quantization noise. Evaluated on single-GPU setups across WILDS benchmarks, SQR achieves a superior throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier compared to static and heuristic mixed-precision baselines.
Experiments: Evaluate SQR on WILDS benchmarks with varying latency budgets and precision settings
Compare SQR to static and heuristic mixed-precision baselines in terms of throughput, accuracy, and latency
Investigate the effect of different covariance estimation methods and spectral decay rates on SQR's performance
Assess the robustness of SQR to distributional shifts and adversarial attacks
Risk Factors and Limitations: SQR's performance may be sensitive to the choice of covariance estimation method and spectral decay rate
The approach may not generalize well to extremely low-latency or high-precision settings
SQR's training-free nature may limit its ability to adapt to complex or dynamic input distributions
